docs.title: Elysium UI
docs.text:
Python UI without the rectangles.

Elysium UI is a Python desktop framework for building borderless, shaped, GPU-accelerated, animated apps. Skia for paths and text, wgpu for compositing and shaders, native shaped windows on macOS, Windows, and Linux. First-class animation. Designer-to-developer wiring via paired .esk skin bundles. AI authoring through the Aether agent.

import elysium as ely

app = ely.App(title="Aurora Clock", identifier="dev.elysium.clock")
win = app.window(transparent=True, title_bar=False,
                 resizable=False, initial_size=(360, 360))
win.set_hit_test_path("M 180 0 A 180 180 0 1 0 180 360 A 180 180 0 1 0 180 0 Z")
win.load_skin("clock.esk/")
app.run()

docs.title: Start with a demo
docs.text:
Four flagship tutorials live in Getting Started. Each ships an app you can run, screenshot, and modify on day one.

  • Aurora Clock: a transparent ellipse clock with an animated arc and breathing aurora glow. Five chapters, 30 minutes.
  • Pomodoro Timer: a rounded-rectangle timer with three modes and a radial progress ring. Four chapters, 25 minutes.
  • Stylized Music Player an elaborate borderless music player in the lineage of late-1990s desktop player skin culture. Eight chapters, 90 minutes.
  • Butterfly Banner: the official Elysium logo treatment. A hybrid Monarch and Blue Morpho butterfly glides down and unfurls the Elysium wordmark. Three chapters, 20 minutes.

docs.title: Install
docs.text:
pip install elysium-ui

Pre-built wheels for CPython 3.10 / 3.11 / 3.12 / 3.13 on macOS (arm64 + x86_64), Windows (x64), and Linux (manylinux x86_64 + aarch64). See Getting Started > Install for the alternate distribution paths (uv, conda-forge, Homebrew, GitHub Releases, Docker).

docs.title: Compare and migrate
docs.text:
• Which Python GUI?: decision guide across Tkinter, PyQt6, PySide6, Kivy, Toga, Flet, Streamlit, dearpygui, wxPython, and customtkinter.
  • Migration guides: coming from Tkinter, PyQt6, PySide6, Kivy, or Flet.

docs.title: The Designer
docs.text: Authoring of .esk skin bundles happens in the standalone Elysium Designer app, which ships as a .app on macOS, .exe on Windows, and AppImage on Linux. The Designer's own documentation lives at designer.elysiumui.com. Start with the Blue Morpho to Monarch butterfly tutorial to learn the texture-transfer pipeline that produces the Elysium logo skin.

docs.title: Reading the pages
docs.text:
Each per-class page follows a consistent shape:

  1. Synopsis.
  2. Status (stable / experimental / internal).
  3. Quick example (10-20 lines that produce a screenshot).
  4. Constructors with parameter tables.
  5. Properties (reactive ones tagged).
  6. Methods grouped (lifecycle / layout / rendering / events / util).
  7. Events / hooks emitted.
  8. Recipes that use this class.
  9. Source link on GitHub.

The auto-generator behind these pages is mkdocstrings; docstring edits in the source code flow directly to the published docs.

docs.title: elysium.accessibility
docs.text: Read system accessibility preferences; publish accessible trees for screen readers.

docs.title: Classes
docs.text: Class Purpose A11yPrefs Dataclass of OS-reported a11y preferences

docs.title: Functions
docs.text: Function Purpose current() Return the current A11yPrefs subscribe(fn) Subscribe to changes

docs.title: A11yPrefs fields
docs.text:
@dataclass
class A11yPrefs:
    reduce_motion: bool
    high_contrast: bool
    invert_colors: bool
    larger_text: bool
    screen_reader_active: bool

docs.title: Per-window helpers
docs.text:
(on the Window class: re-listed here for discoverability)

Method Purpose window.publish_a11y_tree(root_id, nodes) Publish the accessible tree window.publish_a11y_focus(id) Tell the OS which node is focused

docs.title: Auto-rendered details
docs.text:
User-preference plumbing — reduce-motion + high-contrast.

Reads OS-level accessibility prefs and exposes them as Signal-like flags that themes / animations watch. Skins that ship variant trees (variants/reduce_motion.json, variants/high_contrast.json) get auto-applied when the corresponding pref is on.

The reads themselves are cheap and cached, but we hook a 1-second polling timer to catch live changes — every platform has a callback API, but the call sites differ enough that polling is the simplest correct option until we wire each one through the native layer.

docs.title: Role
docs.text: Accessibility roles (accesskit-aligned names) a component reports so screen readers describe it correctly.

docs.title: AccessibleNode dataclass
docs.text: A semantic description of a widget for the accessibility tree (the shape the accesskit bridge consumes). Build one per widget; None fields are omitted from :meth:to_dict.

docs.title: Announcer dataclass
docs.text: A live-region announcer: queues text for a screen reader (polite by default, assertive to interrupt). Route :meth:set_sink to the accesskit bridge in a real app; the in-memory log makes it testable.

docs.title: current() -> A11yPrefs
docs.text: Snapshot of the latest OS-reported prefs.

docs.title: subscribe(fn: Callable[[A11yPrefs], None]) -> Callable[[], None]
docs.text: Run fn(current_prefs) whenever any pref changes. Returns an unsubscribe closure.

docs.title: variant_for(prefs: A11yPrefs | None = None) -> str | None
docs.text: Return the variant key the framework should select for the current prefs, or None for the default variant. Reduce-motion wins over high-contrast when both are on — most apps care more about motion sensitivity.

docs.title: announcer() -> Announcer
docs.text: The process-wide default announcer.

docs.title: announce(text: str, assertive: bool = False) -> None
docs.text: Announce via the default :func:announcer.

docs.title: focus_ring_style(prefs: A11yPrefs | None = None) -> tuple
docs.text: (stroke_width, inflate, alpha) for a focus ring — thicker and fully opaque under high-contrast so keyboard focus is unmistakable.

docs.title: paint_focus_ring(dl, x: float, y: float, w: float, h: float, color, radius: float = 6.0, prefs: A11yPrefs | None = None) -> None
docs.text: Draw a consistent keyboard-focus ring around (x, y, w, h), honouring high-contrast prefs.

docs.title: Aether tools.partial
docs.text: Catalog of every Aether-callable tool. Currently 123 tools across 15 modules. Re-generated on every doc build.

docs.title: Mesh (26)
docs.text:
Tool Side effect Undoable One-line purpose mesh.import n/a n/a Import a .obj / .gltf / .glb and add it as a Mesh3D placement. mesh.import_3d n/a n/a Import a 3D model file (.3ds / .obj / .gltf / .glb / .fbx): the same one-action flow as File → Import 3D Model… in the Designer. Parses the file, registers it in the mesh library under its filename stem, and adds a Mesh3D placement centered on the canvas. Returns the new placement's id, the mesh library name, and the triangle / vertex counts so the caller can confirm the import succeeded. mesh.register_from_file n/a n/a Load a .3ds / .obj / .gltf / .glb / .fbx and register it in the MESH_LIBRARY under name without creating a new placement. Use this on launch when a saved .esk references a named mesh (e.g. 'butterfly') that the fresh process has not yet loaded: calling this rebinds the name so the existing Mesh3D placement starts rendering again. mesh.uv_unwrap n/a n/a Re-generate the UV coordinates of a Mesh3D's underlying mesh using a projection scheme. Modes: - 'planar' : flatten from a viewing axis (yaw,pitch); front-facing surface gets a [0,1]² mapping. - 'planar_xy' : project along +Z onto XY plane. - 'cylindrical' : wrap around the Y axis (u=angle, v=height). - 'spherical' : wrap around origin (u=longitude, v=latitude). Mutates the cached mesh object's vert_uvs in place. Does NOT alter vertices, faces, normals, rigging, or part ids: the model's geometry stays identical. Returns the new per-part UV bbox so callers can re-render or rebuild atlases against it. material.project_photo n/a n/a 'Projection painting': bake a photo onto the mesh from a camera angle (yaw, pitch, dist), then save the result as a UV-aligned albedo atlas and bind it. For each mesh vertex, the camera maps it to a screen-space point in the photo; that pixel becomes the color at the vertex's UV. Triangle interior pixels in the atlas are filled by rasterising the UV triangles with bilinear-sampled photo colors. Geometry untouched. The current camera angle on the placement (mesh_yaw/mesh_pitch) is used by default. material.project_per_part n/a n/a Per-part proportional projection painting. Aligns each named mesh part to a caller-supplied photo bbox, then for every atlas pixel inside that part: finds its 3D position via barycentric coords, normalises that position within the part's 3D screen-space bbox (x,y from a given camera view), and samples the photo at the equivalent normalized position inside the part's photo bbox. Result: the top 1% of the mesh's left wing samples the top 1% of the photo's left wing, and so on: every section of every wing/body lines up proportionally to its counterpart in the reference.

part_photo_bboxes maps mesh part name -> {x,y,w,h} in source-image pixel coords. Defaults supplied for parts that look like wing/body using a simple symmetric split of the photo's opaque bbox (left half = first wing-named part, right half = second wing-named part, central vertical strip = body-named part). When the photo's wing is mirrored relative to the mesh's wing (head-on view swap), pass flip_h_for=[part_names] to mirror that part's photo sampling horizontally. Auto-binds the baked atlas as the placement's albedo. Geometry untouched. | | mesh.read_render_bbox | SideEffect.READ | No | Return the tight bbox (in placement-bbox local pixels) of where the rendered Mesh3D actually occupies space: i.e. the alpha-bbox of the cached mesh PNG, scaled into the placement bbox. Lets a caller stamp paint exactly onto the visible mesh region (e.g. the butterfly's wings + body) instead of guessing in empty bbox space. | | mesh.read_part_render_bbox | SideEffect.READ | No | Return the screen-pixel bbox of EACH named sub-mesh part (Wing_Left, Wing_Right, Body, …) inside the placement's rendered output. Projects every vertex of every part through the placement's current camera (mesh_yaw / mesh_pitch / mesh_dist), maps NDC into the rendered image, then scales into placement-bbox local pixels. Lets a caller crop the reference photo to the EXACT pixel dimensions of a model's wing region before applying: the basis for pixel-perfect transfer. | | mesh.read_parts | SideEffect.READ | No | List the named sub-meshes inside a Mesh3D placement's imported model (e.g. for the butterfly .3ds: Wing_Right, Wing_Left, Body) along with their vertex counts. Returns an empty list for procedural meshes that weren't imported from a file. | | mesh.read_uv_bbox | SideEffect.READ | No | Report the UV bounding box (umin, vmin, umax, vmax) for each sub-mesh of a Mesh3D placement. UV coords are in [0,1] and indicate which region of an albedo texture a part samples: essential for picking a photo crop that lands on the correct anatomy at the right size. | | mesh.set_camera | n/a | n/a | Set yaw / pitch / distance for a Mesh3D placement. | | mesh.toggle_wireframe | n/a | n/a | Toggle wireframe rendering on a Mesh3D placement. | | mesh.render_final | SideEffect.READ | No | Path-traced 'Render Final' for the selected mesh; saves to .elysium/renders/. Returns the absolute output path. | | mesh.render_part_mask | SideEffect.READ | No | Render the named sub-mesh part of a Mesh3D placement to a binary mask sized at the placement's on-canvas pixel dimensions (p.w × p.h). The mask is what the user sees - byte-for-byte identical camera + projection as the real PBR render: so any pixel that's '1' on the mask is a pixel the user sees coloured by that part on the checkerboard canvas. Returns the PNG path + pixel count + canvas-space bbox of the part.

Pass parts=[] (omit part) to get the union mask for the whole mesh. | | mesh.world_to_screen | SideEffect.READ | No | Project a world-space (x, y, z) point through a Mesh3D placement's camera and return the resulting CANVAS (x, y) pixel: i.e. the same coordinate space as the Designer's drag-drop placements. Useful for putting a marker on a known model vertex (wing tip, antenna, etc.). | | mesh.lasso_tip_pct | SideEffect.WRITE | No | Pixel-accurate lasso of the top N % of a Mesh3D placement's named part, ordered tip-first.

Algorithm: render the part to a binary mask at the placement's canvas resolution (so each mask pixel maps 1:1 to a canvas pixel), pick the wing tip as the extreme corner of the mask (auto-detected from the part name unless anchor is given), sort every mask pixel by Euclidean distance to the tip, take the top N %. Returns the ordered list of CANVAS-space (x, y) pixels plus the tip + its canvas coordinate.

Optionally builds a marching-ants Shape placement that outlines those pixels (set create_overlay=True). | | image.lasso_left_wing_tip_pct | SideEffect.WRITE | No | Pixel-accurate lasso on the upper-outer tip of an Image placement's left wing: the Image counterpart of mesh.lasso_tip_pct. Algorithm: alpha + non-white silhouette pass on the source photo, split at the horizontal midpoint to isolate the left wing, find the upper-outer corner (min y+x), sort by Euclidean distance to that tip, take the top N % closest pixels, map them into canvas coords using the placement's current size, optionally emit a marching-ants Shape overlay. | | mesh.transfer_wing_from_reference | SideEffect.WRITE | No | Pattern-preserving transfer of a reference Image's wing onto a Mesh3D placement's wing. The source wing's bbox is cropped from the reference photo and image-warped to the model wing's bbox (preserving spatial pattern), then each model wing pixel reads its color from the warped crop at its bbox-local position. Painted into the placement's PaintMask so the result composites over the mesh immediately.

method='bbox_warp' (default): image-warps the source crop so stripes/spots stay in the right place even when the wings have different shapes. method='sweep' : legacy zig-zag row-major copy (loses pattern; only useful as a comparison baseline).

n_regions controls progress reporting (and the row subdivision used in 'sweep' mode); bbox_warp samples every pixel from the warped image, independent of n_regions.

clear_mask=True wipes the previous PaintMask first so successive transfers don't bleed through. | | mesh.bake_paint_mask_to_uv_albedo | SideEffect.WRITE | No | Back-project a placement's screen-space PaintMask into UV space and bind the result as the named sub-mesh part's albedo map (per-part material slot). Uses the same ray-trace pass as the renderer so each canvas pixel maps to the exact mesh UV the renderer would sample from that pixel.

After baking the tool optionally clears the PaintMask (default ON) so PBR shading takes over from the screen-space overlay: that's what lets mesh.generate_normal_map_from_albedo actually catch light through the PBR shader on subsequent renders. Saves the UV-mapped PNG under ~/.elysium/textures/<placement>_<part>_baked.png. | | mesh.generate_normal_map_from_albedo | SideEffect.WRITE | No | Generate a tangent-space normal map from a placement's UV-mapped albedo texture (the one currently bound for the named part) by running a Sobel gradient on the albedo's luminance, then bind it via material.set_part_texture(slot="normal"). Use after mesh.bake_paint_mask_to_uv_albedo so the normal map is derived from the just-baked Blue Morpho color, giving the PBR shader something to catch light off (iridescence). | | mesh.transfer_polar_with_normal_map | SideEffect.WRITE | No | One-shot pipeline: run polar transfer for Wing_Left, bake the PaintMask into a UV-space albedo for the part, then generate + bind a Sobel-derived normal map so the wing catches light through the PBR shader. This is the full path from a reference photo to a PBR-lit iridescent wing in one menu click. | | mesh.save_landmarks | n/a | n/a | Persist a list of paired source/target landmarks as a JSON preset under ~/.elysium/landmarks/.json so the manual landmark transfer can be replayed across sessions / wings / models. | | mesh.load_landmarks | SideEffect.READ | No | Read a landmark preset saved by mesh.save_landmarks. | | mesh.list_landmark_presets | SideEffect.READ | No | List all saved landmark presets under ~/.elysium/landmarks. | | mesh.transfer_landmark_with_normal_map | SideEffect.WRITE | No | One-shot pipeline using manually placed landmarks: run TPS transfer driven by the supplied landmark pairs, bake the result to a UV-space albedo for the named part, then generate + bind a normal map for PBR-driven iridescence under the studio lighting. | | mesh.transfer_bbox_then_landmark_gaps | SideEffect.WRITE | No | Layered transfer: run bbox_warp first (fills the bulk of the wing with the source pattern's overall layout), then run landmark TPS in paint_only_empty mode to fill only the gaps bbox_warp left transparent (where the source butterfly silhouette didn't cover the warped bbox), then bake to a UV-space albedo and generate a normal map for PBR-driven iridescence.

Needs ≥6 paired landmarks. Use this when the bbox-warp baseline looks good for the wing's central pattern but leaves anatomical regions (wing tip / outer rim / inner cell) uncovered. |

docs.title: Texture (15)
docs.text:
Tool Side effect Undoable One-line purpose texture.extract_from_image n/a n/a Crop/clean an image into a seamless tile in the user's texture library at ~/.elysium/textures/. Pass crop as [x, y, w, h] in source-image pixel coords to extract just that region. saturation and contrast are signed deltas in [-1, 1] applied before tiling. texture.apply_layer n/a n/a Add a texture layer to a placement (multi-layer stack). texture.list_library SideEffect.READ No List every texture in the user's local library along with its pixel dimensions, aspect ratio, and file size. texture.crop_to_match n/a n/a Crop a source image to an arbitrary aspect ratio and resolution, save to the user's texture library, return the tile path. Unlike texture.extract_from_image, this preserves the cropped pixels exactly (no tiling pass) and produces an output of the requested dimensions. Use it after mesh.read_uv_bbox to pick a region of the source photo that matches a sub-mesh's UV bbox aspect ratio. Pass either crop [x,y,w,h] in source-pixel coords, or crop_norm [u_min, v_min, u_max, v_max] in [0,1] coords. If out_w/out_h are given, the cropped region is resized to those dimensions; otherwise the crop's native dimensions are kept. texture.stamp_region n/a n/a Stamp a section of a source image onto a placement's PaintMask at the given target rect (in placement-local coords [0,0] = top-left of the bbox). Equivalent of using the Brush tool with a custom image stamp instead of a solid color: lets a caller build up a complex texture section by section. Pass src_crop [x,y,w,h] in source-pixel coords (defaults to whole image) and dst_rect [x,y,w,h] in placement-bbox px (defaults to (0,0,placement.w,placement.h)). opacity (0..1) controls blend strength. texture.assemble_atlas n/a n/a Build a single albedo atlas image that aligns regions of a source photo to a mesh's UV layout. regions is a list of {src_crop:[x,y,w,h], dst_uv_bbox:[umin,vmin,umax,vmax], flip_v:bool}. For each entry the source crop is extracted from src (in source-image pixel coords), scaled to fit the destination UV bbox (translated to pixel coords in a fresh size×size canvas), and pasted in. flip_v defaults to True (matching the .3ds loader's V-flip so on-screen anatomy is right-side up). Existing destination pixels are overwritten: paint anatomy from background → foreground (wings first, body last). Returns the saved atlas path so it can be bound via material.set_texture. texture.clear_paint_mask n/a n/a Wipe a placement's PaintMask (the screen-space brush overlay) back to fully transparent. Equivalent to selecting the placement, picking the Eraser, and dragging across the entire bbox: but in one tool call. Use to revert all freehand painting on a placement before restarting. texture.read_info SideEffect.READ No Inspect an image file on disk. Returns its size, the tight bounding box of opaque pixels (alpha > 200), and the tight bbox of pixels matching an optional color filter so you can pick crops by content instead of guessing. color_filter is one of: 'blue', 'red', 'iridescent', 'dark', or absent (skip). Returns the centroid of matched pixels too, for easy 'aim here' stamping. texture.generate_pbr_maps n/a n/a Generate a full set of PBR maps (diffuse / albedo, normal, roughness, displacement) from a single flat photograph. Diffuse strips baked highlights/shadows via tone-curve normalisation; normal map comes from luminance gradients (Sobel) encoded as tangent-space RGB; roughness is the inverse of local luminance variance (smooth areas = low roughness); displacement is the photo's luminance. Returns a dict with one path per generated map. texture.transfer_section n/a n/a Strict 1:1 pixel transfer from a source-image region to a target placement's PaintMask (screen-space overlay). The source crop is scaled to EXACTLY the target rect's pixel dimensions, then copied row-by-row top-down so each pixel in the source maps to one pixel in the target.

Use row_start / row_end (in [0,1] of the section) to transfer only a partial vertical band: that's the basis for the progressive top-down workflow: call repeatedly with row_end stepping from 0→1 and screenshot between each call to watch the wing fill in section by section.

The transfer is additive on the PaintMask: existing paint below it is overwritten only in the band being painted. | | lasso.wing_perimeter | SideEffect.WRITE | No | Compute the perimeter polygon of a wing's top vertical band, INSIDE the wing silhouette (not a bbox rectangle). Returns a Shape placement-ready polygon point list following the wing outline at rows in [v_start, v_end] of the wing.

For a Mesh3D placement: projects each face's vertices through the placement's camera, identifies which faces fall in the vertical band, then traces the outer edge of those faces as a polygon: so the lasso hugs the actual wing perimeter at that altitude.

For an Image placement: reads the photo's alpha channel inside source_bbox (in source-image pixel coords), finds opaque pixels in the band, returns left/right extents per row as a polygon outline in placement-local canvas pixels (so the polygon is sized to the on-screen image).

Optionally CREATES a marching-ants Shape placement with that polygon when create_overlay=True. | | texture.transfer_uv_band | n/a | n/a | Copy a horizontal band of pixels from a source image into a Mesh3D placement's UV-mapped albedo texture (the texture that's baked onto the mesh's surface: NOT a screen overlay).

Algorithm: identify the named sub-mesh part's UV bbox in the atlas → compute the atlas pixel rect that corresponds to the part's [v_start, v_end] vertical band → crop the source wing region from src_wing_bbox → scale it to the part's render-pixel dimensions (so each source pixel lands on one render pixel after PBR sampling) → take the [v_start, v_end] vertical band of the scaled crop → resample to the atlas-pixel band → write into the atlas and save. If the placement has no albedo bound yet a fresh transparent atlas of size×size is created. The existing atlas (if any) is preserved in regions outside this band so consecutive calls accumulate.

Per-part UV bboxes come from mesh.read_uv_bbox; pixel dimensions of the part on screen come from mesh.read_part_render_bbox. Both are needed. | | texture.flush_caches | n/a | n/a | Clear every in-memory texture/mesh cache so the next render re-reads files from disk. Use after overwriting a texture in the library: without this, pbr._TEX_CACHE keeps the stale pixels even though the file changed. | | texture.delete_from_library | n/a | n/a | Delete a texture from the user's library. Pass the tile's filename stem (e.g. 'bm_wing'). The tile is unlinked from disk but any placement still referencing it will keep its current binding string: the next render will hit a missing file. Bind a different tile via material.set_texture / material.set_part_texture before deleting. | | texture.paint_mask | n/a | n/a | Paint a brush stroke onto a placement's PaintMask. |

docs.title: Material (5)
docs.text: Tool Side effect Undoable One-line purpose material.set n/a n/a Apply a material to a Mesh3D / PBRSphere placement. Either pick a preset from the material library or pass individual params. material.set_texture n/a n/a Bind an image as a material texture map. slot is albedo / metallic_rough / normal / ao / emissive. material.set_part_texture n/a n/a Bind an albedo texture to ONE named sub-mesh of a Mesh3D placement (use mesh.read_parts to discover names). Lets a user re-skin the wings and the body of an imported model with different tiles. Pass an empty path to clear the binding for that part. material.clear SideEffect.DESTRUCTIVE n/a Wipe every material customization on a placement so it renders as the bare imported model. Clears: all PBR texture slots (albedo / metallic_rough / normal / ao / emissive), all per-part textures, the texture-layer stack, the painted PaintMask, and resets PBR knobs to neutral. Use to revert before restarting a texturing workflow from scratch. material.read SideEffect.READ No Read a placement's material parameters.

docs.title: Animation (11)
docs.text: Tool Side effect Undoable One-line purpose animation.add_state n/a n/a Append an AnimState to a placement. animation.modify_state n/a n/a Patch an existing AnimState by name. animation.delete_state n/a n/a Remove an AnimState by name. animation.play n/a n/a Start the global animation playhead. animation.set_playhead n/a n/a Seek the animation playhead to a wall-clock time. animation.read SideEffect.READ No Dump every AnimState on a placement. animation.add_scene n/a n/a Append a Scene (keyframed pose snapshot) to a placement. Same data model as animation.add_state: alternate name matching the Designer's UI terminology. animation.modify_scene n/a n/a Patch a Scene by name (alias of animation.modify_state). animation.delete_scene n/a n/a Remove a Scene by name (alias of animation.delete_state). animation.set_loop n/a n/a Toggle the Designer's animation-loop mode. When True, the timeline cycles continuously while playing. When False (the default) the timeline plays once and stops at the final scene. Drives the same toggle as the toolbar's ↻ Loop button. animation.read_loop SideEffect.READ No Report whether play-loop is on (continuous) or off (one-shot).

docs.title: Placement (12)
docs.text: Tool Side effect Undoable One-line purpose placement.add n/a n/a Create a new placement on the canvas. kind is one of the catalog entries (Button, Card, Mesh3D, PBRSphere, Image, Label, Slider, Toggle, Shape, Region, ...). placement.remove SideEffect.DESTRUCTIVE n/a Delete a placement by id. placement.move n/a n/a Translate a placement to absolute coordinates. placement.resize n/a n/a Resize a placement to (w, h). placement.rename n/a n/a Rename a placement. placement.select n/a n/a Select a placement by id (so subsequent keyboard nudges, Properties-panel edits, etc. target it). Pass id='' to deselect everything. placement.set_property n/a n/a Set an arbitrary property on a placement. Use this for things like color_fill, font_size, label, image_path, or any kind-specific field. placement.duplicate n/a n/a Clone a placement with an optional offset. placement.bring_forward n/a n/a Move a placement up one Z order. placement.send_backward n/a n/a Move a placement down one Z order. placement.list SideEffect.READ No List every placement on the canvas with id / kind / name / bounds. Read-only: call this after changes to verify state. placement.read SideEffect.READ No Get the full property dump for one placement.

docs.title: Shape (4)
docs.text: Tool Side effect Undoable One-line purpose shape.draw_rect n/a n/a Add a rectangle shape placement. shape.draw_ellipse n/a n/a Add an ellipse / circle shape. shape.draw_path n/a n/a Add a custom SVG-path shape. d uses the path mini-language (M / L / Q / C / Z; no A). shape.boolean_op n/a n/a Run a boolean path op on two or more selected shapes. op is union / intersect / subtract / exclude.

docs.title: Brush (7)
docs.text: Tool Side effect Undoable One-line purpose brush.set_texture n/a n/a Activate a tileable RGBA tile as the current Brush stamp. Either pass name (a tile already in the designer's brush palette / texture library at ~/.elysium/textures/) or path (absolute file path). Once set, the Brush tool paints with that texture instead of brush_color; switch back to solid color with brush.clear_texture. scale (default 1.0) stretches the tile in placement-pixel units: 0.5 = pattern twice as dense, 2.0 = pattern twice as large. brush.clear_texture n/a n/a Return the Brush tool to solid-color mode (paints with brush_color again). brush.slurp n/a n/a Eyedropper a tileable swatch out of a source image and stash it in the brush palette under name. crop is [x, y, w, h] in source-image pixel coords; omit to use the whole image. If seamless is true (default) the tile is feathered/wrapped so it tiles without seams. The new tile is also set as the active brush stamp so the very next brush stroke uses it. brush.list_palette SideEffect.READ No List every brush swatch the designer has cached this session (the in-memory palette plus on-disk tiles under ~/.elysium/textures/). brush.read SideEffect.READ No Report the current brush state: tool, radius, opacity, hardness, solid color, and the active texture stamp (if any) plus its scale. brush.stroke n/a n/a Lay a brush stroke on a placement's PaintMask using the currently active brush state (texture if set, otherwise solid brush_color). Coordinates are in placement-bbox local pixels: [0,0] = top-left of the bbox. from and to define the stroke endpoints; pass equal points for a single dab. Honours brush_radius, brush_opacity, brush_hardness, brush_texture_scale. Additive only: no alpha cutout, never erases the underlying model. brush.set_params n/a n/a Adjust brush size / opacity / hardness / color without switching tools. Any omitted field is left alone. color is [r, g, b] or [r, g, b, a] in 0-255.

docs.title: Code (3)
docs.text: Tool Side effect Undoable One-line purpose code.read_file SideEffect.READ No Read a UTF-8 text file from inside the project root. code.write_file SideEffect.DESTRUCTIVE n/a Write a file inside the project root. mode is 'overwrite' (default, destructive) or 'append'. code.patch n/a n/a Replace the first occurrence of find with replace. Fails when find is not unique unless count is set.

docs.title: Code Link (3)
docs.text: Tool Side effect Undoable One-line purpose codelink.scaffold n/a n/a Append a Python handler stub for a hook into the skin's paired source file. Idempotent. codelink.goto SideEffect.READ No Open the user's editor at the handler for the given hook (scaffolds it first if missing). codelink.read_handler SideEffect.READ No Return the source of the handler function for a hook.

docs.title: Hook (3)
docs.text: Tool Side effect Undoable One-line purpose hook.declare n/a n/a Declare a hook on a placement (event / text / value / state / image / slot / style). For state hooks pass options.states; for value hooks pass options.range. hook.set_accessible n/a n/a Set the accessibility role / label / description on a placement's hook so screen readers (VoiceOver / JAWS / Orca) announce it correctly. hook.read SideEffect.READ No Read the hook + accessibility metadata for a placement.

docs.title: Window (8)
docs.text: Tool Side effect Undoable One-line purpose window.set_chrome n/a n/a Toggle the OS window chrome. shape is 'rect' / 'ellipse' / 'path'; pass path_d when shape='path'. window.set_size n/a n/a Resize the design canvas. window.set_bg n/a n/a Set the window background. color is an [r,g,b,a] 0-255 tuple; pass gradient_end to switch to a linear gradient with angle degrees. window.set_studio n/a n/a Pick one of the lighting studios (see agent.list_studios). Affects every PBR/Mesh3D placement. window.set_theme n/a n/a Switch the active theme (Light / Dark / OLED / Glass / Frost). window.read SideEffect.READ No Dump the current window definition. view.set_zoom n/a n/a Set the canvas (form-area) zoom factor (1.0 = 100%). Equivalent to Cmd+= / Cmd+- / Cmd+0 keyboard shortcuts and trackpad pinch. Clamped to the Designer's zoom limits. view.read_zoom SideEffect.READ No Read the current canvas zoom factor (1.0 = 100%).

docs.title: Snapshot (4)
docs.text: Tool Side effect Undoable One-line purpose snapshot.list SideEffect.READ No List every snapshot in the current session. snapshot.restore SideEffect.DESTRUCTIVE No Roll the project back to the named snapshot. snapshot.diff SideEffect.READ No Unified diff of the document.json between two snapshots. snapshot.branch n/a n/a Label the current point as a branch the user can revisit.

docs.title: Meta (13)
docs.text: Tool Side effect Undoable One-line purpose agent.search_docs SideEffect.READ No Substring-search the framework's docs (under docs/). agent.list_components SideEffect.READ No Return every Elysium component class shipped in v1. agent.list_studios SideEffect.READ No Return every lighting studio preset. agent.list_presets SideEffect.READ No Return every PBR material preset. dev.reload_module n/a n/a Hot-reload a Python module by dotted name (e.g. 'elysium.render.pbr', 'elysium.aether.tools.window'). Uses importlib.reload: fresh calls hit the new code. Tools registered via @register_tool re-register on reload. Module-level state (caches, registries that aren't decorator-driven) is recreated, so caller may also want a follow-up cache flush. Does NOT reload elysium-designer/main.py: the running Designer instance retains its old class methods; for those changes, use dev.reload_designer_module (which monkey-patches the Designer class onto the live instance) or restart. dev.designer_module_info n/a n/a Diagnostic: report what sys.modules key the running Designer's class lives under, plus a sample of candidate module names from sys.modules. dev.dump_placement_attrs n/a n/a Diagnostic: dump raw attribute values + presence on a placement instance, so we can tell whether a hot-reload field addition actually took effect on the live instance. dev.dump_tool n/a n/a Dump runtime info about a registered tool: module, code object id, function id, and a few co_consts strings: so callers can verify hot-reload actually replaced the function reference. dev.reload_designer_module n/a n/a Hot-reload the running Designer's main module and monkey-patch every non-dunder method/function from the fresh module onto the live Designer instance + class, so subsequent calls execute the new code without a restart. Returns the number of attributes patched. Existing in-flight state (placements, selection, animation clock) is preserved. dev.eval n/a n/a Diagnostic-only: eval a Python expression in a context with session, designer, pbr and dp (designer_preview) pre-bound. Returns repr(result) trimmed. dev.thread_dump n/a n/a Capture a quick snapshot of every Python thread's current stack so we can see if the render thread is blocked. dev.probe_designer n/a n/a Quick liveness probe: returns selected attributes/methods on the live Designer class so callers can verify a hot-reload actually patched what they expect. agent.report_capability_gap n/a n/a File a framework feature request the agent has just hit. Persists to ~/.elysium/feedback/.jsonl.

docs.title: Run (6)
docs.text: Tool Side effect Undoable One-line purpose run.start n/a n/a Spawn the user app as a subprocess with ELYSIUM_INSPECTOR=1. run.stop SideEffect.DESTRUCTIVE n/a Stop the running app (SIGTERM). run.snapshot SideEffect.READ No Capture a PNG of the running app's current canvas. Falls back to a Designer-rendered preview when no app is running so the agent can still verify visual state. run.simulate_input n/a n/a Post synthetic mouse / keyboard events into the running app via the Designer's existing input plumbing. Events: {ev: 'click'|'move'|'key', x, y, code?}. run.read_logs SideEffect.READ No Drain stdout / stderr from the running app. run.frame_stats SideEffect.READ No Pull the latest frame timing + hook traffic from the Inspector TCP endpoint of the running app.

docs.title: Tester (3)
docs.text: Tool Side effect Undoable One-line purpose tester.probe SideEffect.READ No Run a bundle of autonomous test scenarios against the running app. Returns a list of findings the agent can act on. Scenarios: 'frame_budget', 'no_tracebacks', 'every_hook_fires', 'screenshot_baseline', 'memory_stable'. tester.set_baseline n/a n/a Lock in the current canvas snapshot as the visual baseline for screenshot_baseline probes. tester.replay n/a n/a Replay a recorded user-input session against the running app for regression / repro purposes.

Aether — the autonomous design + code agent for Elysium UI.

See :doc:/specs/agent for the design spec. This module exposes the public surface: Daemon, Session, Provider, Tool, Snapshot, and the register_tool decorator used to add new operations to the registry.

docs.title: elysium.ai
docs.text: Batch one-shot AI calls: generate a skin from a prompt, modify an existing skin, polish a skin.

docs.title: Functions (async)
docs.text: Function Purpose generate_skin(prompt, hooks=[], size=(960, 540), seed=None) Produce a fresh skin modify_skin(path, prompt, seed=None) Return a SkinDiff magic_polish(path, intensity='balanced') Return a curated SkinDiff

docs.title: Classes
docs.text: Class Purpose GeneratedSkin Result of generate_skin; has .save(path) SkinDiff Result of modify_skin / magic_polish

docs.title: Provider control
docs.text: Function Purpose set_provider(name) "anthropic" / "openai" / "ollama" / "stub" current_provider() Name of the active provider set_cache_ttl(seconds) Cache identical prompts

docs.title: SkinDiff
docs.text:
diff.summary()           # human-readable changes
diff.preview()           # unified diff
diff.changes             # list of (placement_id, field, before, after)
diff.apply(path)         # write changes
diff.apply(path, only=[...])    # selective

docs.title: Auto-rendered details
docs.text:
LLM-backed skin generation, modification, and polish.

Providers are pluggable via the Provider protocol. The Anthropic provider is the default; OpenAI and a deterministic local "stub" provider are also shipped.

from elysium import ai
skin = await ai.generate_skin(
    prompt="A glassmorphic music player with a circular play button.",
    hooks=["play.click", "track.title.text", "progress.value"],
    size=(960, 540),
)
skin.save("skins/generated.esk/")

If no API key is set, the stub provider returns a deterministic procedural skin so tests and the Designer can run offline.

docs.title: AnthropicProvider
docs.text: Calls the Anthropic Messages API. Requires ANTHROPIC_API_KEY in env. Imports the SDK lazily so the dep is optional.

docs.title: StubProvider
docs.text: Offline provider that returns a deterministic procedural skin based on the prompt + requested hooks. Used in tests, in the Designer's offline mode, and as a fallback when no API key is set.

docs.title: save(path: str | os.PathLike) -> Path
docs.text: Write the skin as an unzipped .esk directory.

docs.title: preview() -> str
docs.text: Return a unified-diff string of the JSON serializations.

docs.title: generate_skin(prompt: str, hooks: list[str], size: tuple[int, int] = (960, 540), provider: str | Provider | None = None) -> GeneratedSkin async
docs.text:
Generate a .esk skin from a natural-language prompt.

The provider returns a JSON document that we validate + load into a GeneratedSkin ready to .save(...).

docs.title: modify_skin(skin: dict | str | os.PathLike, prompt: str, provider: str | Provider | None = None) -> SkinDiff async
docs.text: Take an existing skin and apply a natural-language modification. Returns a SkinDiff you can preview / apply / discard.

docs.title: magic_polish(skin: dict | str | os.PathLike, *, provider: str | Provider | None = None, intensity: str = 'balanced') -> SkinDiff async
docs.text: Run the AI 'Magic Polish' pass over a skin — picks restrained tweaks that improve gradient quality, shadow softness, spacing, and animation cues. Returns a SkinDiff.

  • Aether: interactive (vs batch) alternative.

docs.title: elysium.anim
docs.text: Tweens, Timelines, StateMachines, Springs, the AnimationClock, and easings.

docs.title: Animation classes
docs.text: Class Purpose Animation Base for Tween / Timeline Tween Single value interpolation Timeline Sequenced + parallel animations StateMachine Named-state value interpolation Spring Critically-damped natural motion SpringValue Spring as an animation (drives one value) AnimationClock Scheduler for animations

docs.title: Easings
docs.text:
String aliases accepted everywhere easing= appears:

linear, ease_in_quad, ease_out_quad, ease_in_out_quad, ease_in_cubic, ease_out_cubic, ease_in_out_cubic, ease_out_expo, ease_in_sine, ease_out_sine, ease_in_out_sine.

Function Purpose cubic_bezier(p1x, p1y, p2x, p2y) Custom cubic-bezier easing spring(stiffness, damping, mass=1.0) Spring as an easing function easing(name_or_fn) Resolve a name or pass through a callable

docs.title: Loop modes
docs.text: Tween(..., loop="once" | "loop" | "ping_pong").

docs.title: Threading
docs.text: Function Purpose run_animation_thread(clock, fps=60) Spawn a daemon thread that ticks the clock

docs.title: Auto-rendered details
docs.text:
Phase 2.1 animation engine — frame-evaluated tweens, timelines, springs, and state machines.

Architecture:

AnimationClock (one per app)
   └─ tick(dt)  → propagates dt to every active animation
          ├─ Tween:        interpolates one value, calls callback
          ├─ Timeline:     orchestrates many Tweens with offsets
          ├─ StateMachine: drives current_state → animations
          └─ Spring:       physics-based critically damped solver
User code

flap = ely.Tween(0.0, 1.0, duration=2.2, easing="ease-in-out-sine", loop="ping_pong", on_update=lambda v: ...) flap.start(clock) # registers with the global clock clock.tick(dt) # call each render frame

For convenience, ely.run_animation_thread(clock, fn) spawns a 60 Hz thread that ticks clock and calls fn() after each tick — the typical pattern for a "publish a frame" loop.

docs.title: Animation
docs.text: Base class — subclasses override _advance(dt) and report aliveness.

docs.title: Tween
docs.text:
Bases: Animation, Generic[T]

Single-value interpolation.

v = [0.0] def on_update(val): v[0] = val t = Tween(0.0, 10.0, duration=1.0, on_update=on_update) clock = AnimationClock() t.start(clock) clock.tick(0.5) abs(v[0] - 5.0) < 1e-9 True

docs.title: value: T property
docs.text: Current value without advancing — useful for polling display loops.

docs.title: Timeline
docs.text:
Bases: Animation

Orchestrates many Tweens at relative offsets.

values = {} tl = Timeline() tl.add(Tween(0.0, 1.0, duration=1.0, on_update=lambda v: values.update(a=v)), at=0.0) tl.add(Tween(0.0, 1.0, duration=0.5, on_update=lambda v: values.update(b=v)), at=0.5) c = AnimationClock(); tl.start(c) c.tick(0.25); abs(values['a'] - 0.5 ** 3) < 0.2 # ease-out-cubic at t=0.25 True

docs.title: StateMachine dataclass
docs.text:
State graph with named transitions, animated by Tweens authored per (from_state, to_state) pair.

sm = StateMachine("idle", {"idle": {}, "hover": {}}) sm.current == "idle" True sm.transition_to("hover") sm.current == "hover" True

docs.title: Spring dataclass
docs.text: Critically-damped harmonic oscillator. Use via spring() easing or SpringValue for time-uncoupled simulation.

docs.title: SpringValue
docs.text:
Bases: Animation

Time-uncoupled spring — give it a moving target each frame, it chases naturally. Used for cursor-following, etc.

docs.title: AnimationClock
docs.text:
Owns the set of live animations and ticks them in lockstep.

tick(dt) is normally driven by the render loop; tests can call it with explicit dt values for deterministic stepping.

docs.title: tick_realtime() -> float
docs.text: Advance using wall-clock dt since the last call. Returns dt.

docs.title: cubic_bezier(p1x: float, p1y: float, p2x: float, p2y: float) -> Callable[[float], float]
docs.text:
Approximate a CSS-style cubic-bezier easing curve.

Newton iteration on the X parameter; six iterations is enough to be visually indistinguishable for typical UI durations.

docs.title: spring(stiffness: float, damping: float, mass: float = 1.0) -> Callable[[float], float]
docs.text: Closed-form damped harmonic oscillator easing — 0..1.

docs.title: run_animation_thread(clock: AnimationClock, on_frame: Callable[[], None], *, target_hz: float = 60.0, idle_hz: float = 4.0, idle_after: float = 0.6, is_busy: Callable[[], bool] | None = None, running: Callable[[], bool] | None = None) -> threading.Thread
docs.text: Spawn a daemon thread that ticks on_frame() at target_hz while the UI is animating / hovered / pressed, and drops to idle_hz after idle_after seconds with no activity. Pass is_busy=lambda: True to disable idle decay entirely (legacy behaviour).

docs.title: elysium.brush
docs.text: The brush authoring + painting Python API. The Designer's brush GUI sits on top of this.

docs.title: Engine framework
docs.text: Symbol Purpose BrushEngine Base class for engines ParamSpec(name, min, max, default, ...) Declares one engine parameter register_engine(engine) Register a new engine globally get_engine(id) Look up an engine by id list_engines() List every registered engine apply_dynamics(params, dynamics, sample) Apply pressure / tilt / etc. to params

docs.title: Built-in engines
docs.text: Id Class round_stamp RoundStampEngine airbrush AirbrushEngine bristle BristleEngine texture TextureEngine pattern PatternEngine wet_mix WetMixEngine

docs.title: Presets
docs.text: Symbol Purpose Preset Dataclass of engine + params + dynamics + metadata load_preset(path) Load a .elybrush file save_preset(preset, path=None) Save to user library

docs.title: Library
docs.text: Symbol Purpose Library The in-memory preset library library() Get the active Library user_brushes_dir() Path to user brushes builtin_brushes_dir() Path to bundled brushes ensure_user_dir_seeded() Create user dir with starter brushes reload_with_skin(path) Refresh library when active skin changes

docs.title: Importers
docs.text: Function Purpose import_abr(path) Photoshop .abr → list of Preset import_sut(path) Clip Studio .sut → list of Preset

docs.title: Auto-rendered details
docs.text:
Public brush API.

This package is the Phase A foundation for the Procreate + CSP hybrid brush system. See docs/brush-palette-plan.md for the full design.

The public surface is intentionally small:

  • register_engine / get_engine / list_engines — the engine registry. Each engines/<name>.py module calls register_engine at import time.
  • library() — singleton Library that walks the on-disk preset directories and indexes everything for fast lookup + search.
  • Preset / load_preset / save_preset — preset record IO.
  • user_brushes_dir / builtin_brushes_dir — path resolution.

Importing this module triggers a side-effect: every engine in engines/ is imported so its register_engine(...) call fires. That way callers can from elysium import brush and immediately have all engines available without needing to know their names.

docs.title: BrushEngine
docs.text:
Subclass once per engine. A subclass must:

  • Set ID to a stable string used as the preset["engine"] field. e.g. "RoundStamp" / "WetMix" / "Bristle".
  • Declare PARAM_SCHEMA — the list of every tunable parameter. Each entry is a ParamSpec.
  • Implement stamp(mask, x, y, params, erase) and stroke(mask, x0, y0, x1, y1, params, erase). Both write pixels into the PaintMask using whatever algorithm the engine owns. The base class provides defaults that call straight into PaintMask.stamp / stroke — sufficient for the baseline RoundStamp behavior.

Engines must be deterministic given the same (samples, params) inputs so per-stroke undo can replay them without surprises. Any pseudo-randomness (jitter, hue drift) must be seeded by the sample's stamp_index so two identical strokes produce identical pixel output.

docs.title: defaults() -> dict
docs.text: Return the default param dict — every spec's default value keyed by its name. Presets only need to override the params they care about; missing keys fall through to these.

docs.title: resolve(preset_params: dict | None) -> dict
docs.text: Merge a preset's params dict on top of the defaults so the engine never sees a missing key. Returns a fresh dict (no mutation of inputs).

docs.title: stamp(mask, x: float, y: float, params: dict, erase: bool = False) -> None
docs.text: Apply a single dab at (x, y) in mask-local coordinates. Subclasses override to add jitter, multi-strand bristles, wet blending, etc. Base implementation = the default PaintMask stamp call, exactly matching the pre-engine behavior.

docs.title: stroke(mask, x0: float, y0: float, x1: float, y1: float, params: dict, erase: bool = False) -> None
docs.text: Apply a continuous stroke segment from (x0, y0) to (x1, y1). Base implementation calls PaintMask's segment rasteriser so the default behavior is pixel-identical to the pre-engine brush code path.

docs.title: ParamSpec dataclass
docs.text: One row in an engine's parameter schema.

docs.title: to_dict() -> dict
docs.text: Serialize to a JSON-safe dict. The leading-underscore _path field is omitted so reloads from disk don't pin a stale on-disk location into the JSON itself.

docs.title: from_dict(d: dict, path: str = '') -> 'Preset' classmethod
docs.text: Tolerant loader — unknown fields are ignored, missing fields fall back to dataclass defaults so old preset files survive schema additions.

docs.title: Library
docs.text:
In-memory snapshot of every available preset. Holds two indexes:

  • by_id — dict[preset_id, Preset]
  • by_category — dict[category_path, list[Preset]] (insertion-ordered)

Plus a token-based search index so the Library Modal's search box filters thousands of presets in O(1) per token.

docs.title: search(query: str) -> list[Preset]
docs.text: Tokenise the query, intersect each token's posting list, return matching presets in stable order. Empty query → all.

docs.title: load(*dirs: Path | str) -> None
docs.text: Walk each directory recursively, loading every *.json as a Preset. Later directories override earlier ones by preset ID, so the user-global dir overlays builtin defaults and a per-skin brushes/ override beats the user-global one.

docs.title: reload(*dirs: Path | str) -> None
docs.text: Drop the existing index + reload from disk. Use on hot-reload of brush files.

docs.title: list_engines() -> list[BrushEngine]
docs.text: Snapshot of every registered engine — order is insertion order (stable, so the UI lists them consistently across reloads).

docs.title: register_engine(engine: BrushEngine) -> None
docs.text: Add an engine instance to the global registry. Called by each engines/<name>.py module at import time. Duplicate IDs overwrite silently so hot-reload of an engine module replaces the live instance.

docs.title: apply_dynamics(params: dict, dynamics: dict | None, pressure: float = 1.0, velocity: float = 0.0) -> dict
docs.text:
Multiply per-input curves onto the base params.

Returns a NEW dict (no mutation of the caller's params). The inputs map to standard Designer touch input:

  • pressure — 0..1, sampled from WM_POINTER per stamp (1.0 when the native layer hasn't reported pressure yet so the renderer falls back to "full press" semantics).
  • velocity — 0..1, normalised stroke speed; 0 = stationary, 1 = max-speed drag.

Curve keys recognised by name: pressure_<target>, velocity_<target> where <target> is size, opacity, or spacing. Tilt is intentionally absent — Windows touch screens don't report tilt, and surfacing a dial that does nothing would mislead the user.

Missing curves and flat curves (all y == 1) are no-ops so this function is safe to call unconditionally — the per-stamp cost is a single next() over the dynamics dict.

docs.title: save_preset(preset: Preset, path: str | Path | None = None) -> Path
docs.text: Write a preset JSON to disk. If path is omitted, uses the preset's existing _path (so save-in-place works); otherwise the new path is recorded so subsequent saves go there too.

docs.title: reload_with_skin(skin_path: Path | str | None) -> Library
docs.text: Re-walk the user-global dir + the per-skin override dir (if present). Called by the Designer when a new .esk is opened so a skin-specific brush set takes effect immediately.

docs.title: user_brushes_dir() -> Path
docs.text: OS-specific user brush directory. Returns the path even when the directory doesn't exist yet — callers are expected to mkdir.

docs.title: builtin_brushes_dir() -> Path
docs.text: Directory inside the installed package containing the starter presets. These get copied into user_brushes_dir() on first launch so the user can edit them without touching the source.

docs.title: ensure_user_dir_seeded() -> Path
docs.text:
Create the user brush directory if missing AND copy every builtin preset into it on first launch. Returns the user dir.

Re-running this does NOT clobber files that already exist — shutil.copy2 is invoked only when the destination is missing, so user edits to a seeded builtin survive subsequent launches.

docs.title: import_brush_file(path: str | Path, assets_dir: str | Path | None = None) -> list[Preset]
docs.text:
Parse path and return the Presets it contains. The returned Presets are NOT yet on disk — callers should call save_imported_presets() to actually populate the library.

Stamp images extracted from .abr/.sut/.elybrush land in assets_dir (default: a sibling imported_assets/ folder).

docs.title: save_imported_presets(presets: list[Preset], user_dir: Path) -> list[Path]
docs.text: Write each imported preset as its own JSON file under user_dir/<engine_slug>/<preset_slug>.json. Returns the list of paths written so callers can show "imported N brushes" status.

docs.title: write_elybrush(preset: Preset, dst: str | Path, stamp_image_path: str | Path | None = None, thumbnail_path: str | Path | None = None) -> Path
docs.text: Pack a Preset (plus optional stamp + thumbnail images) into a single .elybrush archive at dst. Returns the written path.

docs.title: read_elybrush(src: str | Path, extract_to: str | Path | None = None) -> list[Preset]
docs.text: Read a .elybrush (single preset) or .elybrush-set (zip of multiple .elybrush). Extracts any stamp/thumbnail images into extract_to (defaults to a sibling assets/ dir of the source) and rewrites preset.params["pattern_path"] to point at the extracted file. Returns the list of Presets ready to register with the Library.

  • Designer > Brush system

docs.title: elysium.charts
docs.text: Charts — LineChart, AreaChart (stacked), BarChart, DonutChart/PieChart, Sparkline and Legend, plus nice_ticks and the format_money/format_pct/format_compact helpers. The equivalent of QtCharts, drawn on the DisplayList.

docs.title: Auto-rendered details
docs.text:
Charts — data visualization for dashboards (Qt's QtCharts class of widget).

Immediate-mode chart components that paint onto a DisplayList using only the existing path primitives (stroke_path for polylines, fill_path with SVG arc A commands for donut wedges), so there is no native dependency. Every chart reads current_theme() at paint time and recolours with the theme.

Components: :class:Sparkline, :class:LineChart / :class:AreaChart, :class:BarChart, :class:DonutChart / :class:PieChart, and a :class:Legend. Data goes in as :class:Series. Number formatting helpers (:func:format_money, :func:format_pct, :func:format_compact) keep axis and label text consistent.

docs.title: Series dataclass
docs.text: One data series. values are y-values (x is the index); color is optional (the palette fills in).

docs.title: AreaChart dataclass
docs.text:
Bases: LineChart

A :class:LineChart with the area filled (and optionally stacked).

docs.title: DonutChart dataclass
docs.text: A donut (or pie when inner_ratio == 0). segments are (label, value, color|None); an optional centre label/value is shown.

docs.title: Legend dataclass
docs.text: entries are (label, color|None) or (label, color, value_str); laid out as one row per entry with a swatch.

docs.title: chart_palette(t: Any = None) -> list[Color]
docs.text: A categorical palette ordered for good adjacent contrast. Finance-leaning (the COGS / ad-spend / refunds / shipping / fees accent family) but theme primary/accent lead so charts feel native to the active theme.

docs.title: nice_ticks(lo: float, hi: float, target: int = 5) -> tuple[float, float, float]
docs.text: Return (nice_lo, nice_hi, step) covering [lo, hi] with ~target round ticks (1/2/5 × 10ⁿ).

docs.title: format_compact(v: float) -> str
docs.text: 12500 → 12.5k; 3_400_000 → 3.4M.

docs.title: Subcommands

docs.title: Programmatic dispatch
docs.text:
from elysium.cli import dispatch
dispatch(["dev", "myapp.py"])     # equivalent to `elysium dev myapp.py`

Useful for testing or embedding the CLI in larger tooling.

docs.text: Code Meaning 0 Success 1 Generic error 2 Bad usage 3 Native lib missing 4 AI provider error 5 Build / packaging error

docs.title: elysium.codelink
docs.text: Two-way wiring between the Designer and your Python source. Same one-click feel as Interface Builder in Xcode, or the Forms designer in Visual Studio.

docs.title: Functions
docs.text: Function Purpose index_handlers(path, known_hooks) Map handler-name → location scaffold_handler(path, hook, window_var) Insert a stub if missing goto_handler(path, hook, known_hooks, window_var) Open editor at handler; scaffold if needed pair_file(skin_path, py_path) Persist the pairing on the skin resolve_paired_file(skin_path) Read back a pairing

docs.title: Editor detection
docs.text: The launcher walks $ELYSIUM_EDITOR → $VISUAL → $EDITOR, then probes PATH for VS Code, Cursor, Windsurf, Zed, Sublime, Helix, PyCharm, IntelliJ, Neovim, Vim, Emacs, and Xed in that order.

docs.title: Dataclasses
docs.text: Class Purpose HandlerLocation(line, function, ...) Where a handler lives in a file

docs.title: Auto-rendered details
docs.text:
Two-way Designer ↔ code wiring.

Given a Python source file paired with a .esk, this module:

  • Indexes every hook handler — declarations like @win.on("play.click") / @window.on("play.click") and def on_play_click(...) / def play_click(...).
  • Opens the user's editor at the handler's line via the right CLI for VS Code / Cursor / PyCharm / Sublime / Neovim / Helix / Zed / Xcode, or the system default as a fallback.
  • Scaffolds a stub when no handler exists yet — appends a decorated function at the bottom of the file and returns the new line number.

The convention follows the framework's docs::

@win.on("play.click")
def on_play_click(): ...

Both forms are recognised; the scaffolder writes the decorator form because it's the lowest-friction wiring for a developer reading the file cold.

docs.title: HandlerLocation dataclass
docs.text: Where a single hook is implemented in a Python file.

docs.title: index_handlers(source: str | Path, known_hooks: Iterable[str] = ()) -> dict[str, HandlerLocation]
docs.text:
Return a map hook_name -> HandlerLocation for one .py file.

Walks top-level + class-level functions. The naming-convention fallback only fires when known_hooks is supplied so we don't invent associations for arbitrary functions.

docs.title: scaffold_handler(source: str | Path, hook: str, window_var: str = 'win') -> HandlerLocation
docs.text: Append a stub handler for hook to source and return the location of the new function. Idempotent — if the file already declares a handler for hook, returns the existing location.

docs.title: detect_editor() -> EditorSpec | None
docs.text:
Return the first editor available on PATH, honoring $ELYSIUM_EDITOR

$VISUAL > $EDITOR overrides.

docs.title: open_in_editor(file: str | Path, line: int = 1, col: int = 1) -> bool
docs.text: Open file at the given line (1-indexed) in the user's editor. Returns True on a successful spawn.

docs.title: goto_handler(source: str | Path, hook: str, *, scaffold_if_missing: bool = True, known_hooks: Iterable[str] = (), window_var: str = 'win') -> HandlerLocation | None
docs.text: Open the editor at the handler for hook in source, scaffolding the stub when it doesn't exist yet.
